title: Zaalouk
subtitle: Moroccan cooked aubergine and tomato salad
excerpt: Aubergine and tomato cooked down together with garlic, cumin, paprika, and coriander until soft and spreadable, then mashed roughly and served warm or at room temperature as a dip or side dish. One of the essential Moroccan salad plates that precede the main course.
sourceNotes: Zaalouk is one of the cooked salads that form the first course of the Moroccan meal. Along with taktouka (green pepper and tomato) and carrot salad, it appears on every Moroccan table before the main dish. The cooked-down aubergine and tomato combination is found across the Maghreb and the Middle East under various names.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps refrigerated for 3 days. Serve at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made a few hours ahead — the flavour deepens as it sits.

content.text: The aubergine can be cooked two ways: roasted whole in the oven until collapsed (more intense, smoky flavour) or diced and cooked directly in the pan with the tomatoes (faster, less smoky). The roasted method takes longer but gives a better result. Both are correct versions.

The final texture should be rough and spreadable, not a smooth purée. Mash with a fork rather than blending — zaalouk should retain some texture. A generous pour of olive oil at the end, once off the heat, is not optional.

Heat 2 tablespoons of the {{olive-oil}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the diced {{aubergine}} and cook, stirring occasionally, for 10 to 12 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Stir and cook over medium heat, stirring frequently and breaking up the tomatoes, for 15 minutes until the mixture has thickened and dried out. Mash roughly with a fork to a coarse paste, leaving some texture. Remove from the heat. Stir in the remaining {{olive-oil}}, {{lemon-juice}}, and {{coriander}}. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve warm or at room temperature.

items.fix: Continue cooking uncovered until the mixture dries out and the oil begins to separate. It should look shiny and thick, not soupy
Salting cut aubergine and leaving for 20 minutes before rinsing reduces bitterness, though modern aubergines are less bitter than older varieties. The cooking should fully soften the flesh

items.cause: Insufficient spice or no fresh herbs
items.fix: Cumin is the lead spice — taste and add more if needed. The fresh coriander at the end is essential for brightness

content.text: Zaalouk sits alongside taktouka, carrot salad, and briouats as one of the Moroccan salad course dishes served before the main event. Moroccan meals traditionally begin with an array of small cooked salads; zaalouk is a constant. It is scooped with bread and eaten as a starter or alongside grilled meat. In the home it doubles as a quick weeknight side dish for grilled fish or chicken, and it keeps well for a few days in the fridge.
